architecture_summary: InstructBLIP is a vision-language instruction tuning framework built on pretrained BLIP-2 models, where the Q-Former is finetuned while the image encoder and LLM are frozen. It adds instruction-aware visual feature extraction by feeding instruction text into the Q-Former, and uses balanced dataset sampling during instruction tuning.

benchmark_results.table_caption: Table 1: Zero-shot results on the held-out datasets. Here, Visdial, HM and SciQA denote the Visual Dialog, HatefulMemes and ScienceQA datasets, respectively. For ScienceQA, we only evaluate on the set with image context. Following previous works [4, 49, 32], we report the CIDEr score [42] for NoCaps and Flickr30K, iVQA accuracy for iVQA, AUC score for HatefulMemes, and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) for Visual Dialog. For all other datasets, we report the top-1 accuracy (%).

novel_contributions: A systematic and comprehensive study on vision-language instruction tuning using 26 publicly available datasets transformed into instruction tuning format
An instruction-aware Query Transformer that takes instruction text as additional input to extract task-relevant visual features
A balanced sampling strategy that samples datasets with probabilities proportional to the square root of their sizes
A suite of InstructBLIP models using FlanT5 and Vicuna backbones, open-sourced by the authors

key_claims.claim: InstructBLIP attains state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across all 13 held-out datasets and substantially outperforms BLIP-2 and larger Flamingo models.
The paper introduces an instruction-aware Query Transformer that extracts informative features tailored to the given instruction.
The paper transforms 26 publicly available datasets into instruction tuning format and uses 13 held-in datasets for instruction tuning and 13 held-out datasets for zero-shot evaluation.
Balanced dataset sampling improves overall performance for both held-in evaluation and held-out generalization.
InstructBLIP achieves state-of-the-art finetuning performance on downstream tasks such as ScienceQA, OCR-VQA, and A-OKVQA.
